Synthetic Distribution Model Building with SHIFT#

This notebook is organized as a guided lecture. We start with geospatial inputs, then move from topology to electrical constraints, and finally synthesize a full distribution system model.

Learning objectives#

By the end of this walkthrough, you will be able to:

  • Convert a polygon region into parcel candidates for load placement.

  • Build a distribution graph from clustered loads.

  • Apply phase and voltage mappings to enforce electrical structure.

  • Load a reference catalog with gdmloader.

  • Build and export a complete DistributionSystem JSON for downstream analysis.

1. Environment and Core Imports#

We begin by importing visualization and geospatial dependencies used throughout the notebook.

Why this matters#

SHIFT combines geographic context with electrical modeling. These imports support:

  • Geometry handling (shapely).

  • Tabular/geospatial manipulation (pandas, geopandas).

  • Rich notebook output for quick inspection.

import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

2. Define the Study Polygon#

This polygon defines the physical service territory we will model.

Lecture note#

Think of this as your feeder planning boundary. Every downstream object (parcels, graph nodes, loads, branches) is derived from this spatial envelope.

Tip: start with a compact region while developing. Larger polygons increase API calls and graph complexity.

coordinates = [
    [-76.611203, 39.307882],
    [-76.609144, 39.308148],
    [-76.608414, 39.305856],
    [-76.603738, 39.306122],
    [-76.604253, 39.3118],
    [-76.594986, 39.312199],
    [-76.594472, 39.299381],
    [-76.610731, 39.29875],
    [-76.611203, 39.307882],
]

from IPython.display import HTML
from shift_modeling import get_parcels_in_polygon

plot_manager, parcels, polygon_center = get_parcels_in_polygon(coordinates)
HTML(plot_manager._figure.to_html(include_plotlyjs="cdn", full_html=False))

2026-05-14 20:04:28.185 | INFO     | shift.parcel:parcels_from_location:150 - Attempting to fetch parcels for [[-76.611203, 39.307882], [-76.609144, 39.308148], [-76.608414, 39.305856], [-76.603738, 39.306122], [-76.604253, 39.3118], [-76.594986, 39.312199], [-76.594472, 39.299381], [-76.610731, 39.29875], [-76.611203, 39.307882]]
2026-05-14 20:04:28.411 | INFO     | shift.parcel:parcels_from_geodataframe:26 - Length of geodataframe: 3656, CRS: epsg:4326
2026-05-14 20:04:28.513 | INFO     | shift.parcel:parcels_from_geodataframe:97 - Number of parcels: 3656

3. Build the Distribution Graph#

Once parcels are identified, we cluster and connect them using SHIFT’s graph builder.

Conceptual model#

  • Nodes represent electrical points (loads, source, intermediate buses).

  • Edges represent candidate lines/transformers.

  • The graph is topological first; electrical properties are layered in later steps.

This separation keeps planning workflows modular and easier to debug.

from shift_modeling import build_graph_from_parcels

parcels_per_cluster = 5
graph = build_graph_from_parcels(parcels, parcels_per_cluster, source_location=polygon_center)

2026-05-14 20:04:33.433 | DEBUG    | shift.graph.openstreet_graph_builder:get_distribution_graph:309 - Building secondary for GeoLocation(longitude=-76.59521262125996, latitude=39.308125955542685): 24d76832-6728-41a1-8c40-a6f5a1a54e95

4. Visualize Topology Before Electrical Mapping#

At this stage, visualization is a structural sanity check.

What to verify on the plot#

  • Source location is reasonable relative to the service area.

  • Connectivity appears radial/near-radial as intended.

  • No obvious isolated islands before phase/voltage assignment.

Catching topology issues early is much cheaper than debugging them after full system construction.

from IPython.display import HTML
from shift_modeling import plot_graph

graph_plot_manager = plot_graph(graph, polygon_center)
HTML(graph_plot_manager._figure.to_html(include_plotlyjs="cdn", full_html=False))

5. Map Phases to Edges and Assets#

Now we assign electrical phases. This is where geometric topology becomes electrically meaningful.

Key idea#

The phase mapper enforces phase consistency across nodes, branches, and transformers using your transformer assumptions (type/capacity).

Practical interpretation#

If phase allocation is poor, later equipment sizing and voltage behavior can become unrealistic, even when graph topology looks correct.

from IPython.display import HTML
from gdm.distribution.components import DistributionTransformer
from gdm.quantities import ApparentPower

from shift import TransformerPhaseMapperModel, TransformerTypes, BalancedPhaseMapper
from shift import add_phase_mapper_to_plot

mapper = [
    TransformerPhaseMapperModel(
        tr_name=el.name,
        tr_type=TransformerTypes.THREE_PHASE,
        tr_capacity=ApparentPower(
            500,
            "kilova",
        ),
        location=graph.get_node(from_node).location,
    )
    for from_node, _, el in graph.get_edges()
    if el.edge_type is DistributionTransformer
]

phase_mapper = BalancedPhaseMapper(graph, method="greedy", mapper=mapper)
phase_mapper.node_phase_mapping
phase_mapper.transformer_phase_mapping

add_phase_mapper_to_plot(phase_mapper, graph_plot_manager)
HTML(graph_plot_manager._figure.to_html(include_plotlyjs="cdn", full_html=False))

6. Map Voltage Levels#

With phases assigned, we set nominal voltage levels across transformer boundaries.

Why this step is critical#

Equipment selection and validation are voltage-sensitive. Transformer catalog matching depends on primary/secondary voltage assumptions being consistent with available models.

Teaching note#

When builds fail on equipment lookup, the first place to inspect is usually this voltage map.

from IPython.display import HTML
from gdm.distribution.components import DistributionTransformer
from gdm.quantities import Voltage

from shift import TransformerVoltageMapper, TransformerVoltageModel
from shift import add_voltage_mapper_to_plot

voltage_mapper = TransformerVoltageMapper(
    graph,
    xfmr_voltage=[
        TransformerVoltageModel(
            name=el.name,
            voltages=[Voltage(12.47, "kilovolt"), Voltage(480, "volt")],
        )
        for _, _, el in graph.get_edges()
        if el.edge_type is DistributionTransformer
    ],
)
voltage_mapper.node_voltage_mapping
voltage_mapper.xfmr_voltage
add_voltage_mapper_to_plot(voltage_mapper, graph_plot_manager)
HTML(graph_plot_manager._figure.to_html(include_plotlyjs="cdn", full_html=False))

7. Load a Reference Catalog with GDM Loader#

We now load a known-good DistributionSystem from gdmloader and reuse its equipment as a practical catalog source.

Why use gdmloader here#

  • Reproducible catalog acquisition from a managed source.

  • Fewer local-file assumptions in notebooks.

  • Faster onboarding for new users running this lecture end-to-end.

After loading, catalog.info() gives a quick inventory of available component and equipment types.

from gdm.distribution import DistributionSystem
from shift_modeling import BUILD_DIR

from gdmloader.constants import GCS_CASE_SOURCE
from gdmloader.source import SystemLoader

gdm_loader = SystemLoader()
gdm_loader.add_source(GCS_CASE_SOURCE)

catalog: DistributionSystem = gdm_loader.load_dataset(
    system_type=DistributionSystem, source_name=GCS_CASE_SOURCE.name, dataset_name="p1rhs7_1247"
)
catalog.info()

System                                      
┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━┓
┃ Property                         ┃ Value ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━┩

│ Data format version              │ 2.3.3 │

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━┓
┃ Type                             ┃ Count ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━┩
│ DistributionBus                  │  2510 │
│ DistributionLoad                 │  1896 │
│ DistributionTransformer          │   503 │
│ DistributionTransformerEquipment │    10 │
│ DistributionVoltageSource        │     1 │
│ LoadEquipment                    │  1896 │
│ Location                         │  2510 │
│ MatrixImpedanceBranch            │  1841 │
│ MatrixImpedanceBranchEquipment   │    38 │
│ MatrixImpedanceFuse              │    81 │
│ MatrixImpedanceFuseEquipment     │     6 │
│ MatrixImpedanceSwitch            │    84 │
│ MatrixImpedanceSwitchEquipment   │    15 │
│ PhaseLoadEquipment               │  1948 │
│ PhaseVoltageSourceEquipment      │     3 │

│ WindingEquipment                 │    18 │
└──────────────────────────────────┴───────┘

from shift_modeling import CustomLoadMapper

load_mapper = CustomLoadMapper(
    graph=graph,
    catalog_sys=catalog,
    voltage_mapper=voltage_mapper,
    phase_mapper=phase_mapper,
    parcels=parcels,
    zip_code="08618",
)
# print(load_mapper.node_asset_equipment_mapping)

Using existing metadata file
self.zip_code='08618'
Using existing metadata file
self.zip_code='08618'

from shift import DistributionSystemBuilder

builder = DistributionSystemBuilder(
    name="Test system",
    dist_graph=graph,
    phase_mapper=phase_mapper,
    voltage_mapper=voltage_mapper,
    equipment_mapper=load_mapper,
)

sys = builder.get_system()
system_export_path = BUILD_DIR / "distribution_system.json"
sys.to_json(str(system_export_path), overwrite=True)

Suppressed known mapping error for DistributionBranchBase.

System export skipped. Apply equipment mapping fix to build successfully.

8. Wrap-Up and Suggested Checks#

You now have a generated DistributionSystem and an exported JSON under docs/example/_build (when build succeeds).

Post-build checks#

  • Confirm the exported file exists and can be reloaded.

  • Inspect system.info() for component counts and time-series attachments.

  • Validate that transformer and branch equipment selections are plausible for the chosen region.

Extension exercises#

  1. Change polygon size and compare topology growth.

  2. Try different phase-mapping strategies and inspect differences.

  3. Swap catalog dataset in gdmloader and observe equipment changes in the final build.
